text: The surgeon begins perigastric dissection on the lesser curve, entering the lesser sac using blunt dissection. A 60mm blue load is fired horizontally from the right anterior X-ray line through a 12mm port, followed by completion of the posterior pouch dissection.

The surgeon inserts a second blue load from the left midclavicular line through a twelve millimeter port, firing it vertically parallel to the lesser curve. The load reaches the angle of His, completing the gastric pouch. The ligament of Treitz is identified, and the proximal jejunum is measured at thirty to forty centimeters, brought up in an omega loop configuration. A window is created in the mesentery, and a four-inch Penrose drain with two slits is introduced. The loop is passed through the proximal slit, and the Penrose is secured to the liver retractor. A gastrotomy is made at the tip of the gastric pouch, with a matching enterotomy in the small bowel.

A sixty millimeter blue load stapler is partially fired from the left side, creating a three-centimeter staple line that narrows to two to two and a half centimeters after closure of the gastroenterotomy. The hitch is removed, and the window is revised to minimize any candy cane formation. A white load stapler is then fired vertically parallel to the anastomosis. The gastrointestinal anastomosis is closed in two layers using a running 3-0 V-Loc absorbable suture.

The surgeon runs a suture backwards to create a second layer over the staple line, ensuring coverage and overlap with the first inner line. The suture starts at the corner to prevent gaps, and knots are added for security despite using a barb suture. A tube is passed across the anastomosis to verify the posterior wall is not caught, followed by measuring the small bowel with a six-inch tape measure.

The surgeon measures the bowel length using a fifteen-centimeter suture, repeating the measurement multiple times to reach approximately one hundred and fifty centimeters. Monopolar scissors are used to make enterotomies at the biliopancreatic and glulam sites. A sixty-millimeter wide stapler load is introduced from the left side, moving inferior to superior, to prevent narrowing near the jejunal jejunostomy at the glulam side. The defect is closed distally to avoid angulation issues, using various closure techniques including horizontal, vertical, and longitudinal suturing.

The surgeon sutures the blind end of the biliopancreatic limb using trio silk in a Roland's fashion, ensuring the closure does not create angulation that could lead to obstruction at the jejunostomy. The pseudo-Peterson space is closed with two-row ethobon in a purse-string fashion, incorporating part of the omentum and appendageal fat, focusing on the lower portion where hernias typically occur.
